news_summary: Recent breakthroughs reveal new insights into evolution, advanced superconductivity, precise measurements of gravity, and deeper understanding of the quantum realm.
news_summary_fr: Des avancées récentes apportent un nouvel éclairage sur l'évolution, la supraconductivité de pointe, les mesures précises de la gravité et une meilleure compréhension du domaine quantique.
news_summary_es: Los últimos avances aportan nuevos conocimientos sobre la evolución, la superconductividad avanzada, las mediciones precisas de la gravedad y una comprensión más profunda del ámbito cuántico.

articles.title: This 100 million-year-old snake had hind legs and a lost bone that changes evolution
articles.summary: Nearly 100 million years ago, snakes weren’t the sleek, limbless creatures we know today—they still had hind legs and even a cheekbone that has almost vanished in modern species. A remarkably preserved fossil of Najash rionegrina from Argentina has reshaped how scientists think about snake origins, suggesting early snakes were large, wide-mouthed predators rather than tiny burrowers.
articles.content:
A remarkably preserved fossil from Argentina is helping scientists sharpen the picture of how snakes evolved. The specimen belongs to Najash rionegrina, an ancient rear-limbed snake that lived nearly 100 million years ago. Its skull shows that these early snakes still had a cheekbone, also called the jugal bone, a feature that has almost completely disappeared in living snakes. The 2019 study added an important piece to a fossil record that had long been too sparse to clearly explain the earliest stages of snake evolution.

The findings also challenged a popular older idea about snake origins. Instead of beginning as small burrowers, the evidence from Najash pointed to ancestors of modern snakes that were larger-bodied animals with wide mouths. The fossils also showed that early snakes held onto their hindlimbs for a long time before the rise of the mostly limbless snakes alive today.

"Our findings support the idea that the ancestors of modern snakes were big-bodied and big-mouthed -- instead of small burrowing forms as previously thought," explained Fernando Garberoglio, from the Fundación Azara at Universidad Maimónides, in Buenos Aires, Argentina and lead author on the study. "The study also reveals that early snakes retained their hindlimbs for an extended period of time before the origin of modern snakes which are for the most part, completely limbless."

Hidden skull details inside a 100 million year old fossil

The fossil snakes described in the study came from Northern Patagonia and are closely tied to an ancient southern lineage that lived across the continents of Gondwana. Researchers say that group appears to be related to only a small number of unusual snakes still living today. To see inside the specimen without damaging it, the team used micro-computed tomography (micro-CT) scanning. That let them reconstruct the skull in exceptional detail, including the paths of nerves and blood vessels as well as bones buried inside the rock.

That level of detail helped resolve a long-running anatomical debate. Scientists had misunderstood the jugal bone in snakes and snake relatives for generations, and the Najash fossils gave them direct evidence to correct the record. The study's authors argued that these new skulls and skeletons clarified the sequence of bone loss that eventually produced the highly specialized skulls of modern snakes.

"This research revolutionizes our understanding of the jugal bone in snake and non-snake lizards," said Michael Caldwell, professor in the Department of Biological Sciences and Earth and Atmospheric Sciences, and a co-author on the study. "After 160 years of getting it wrong, this paper corrects this very important feature based not on guesswork, but on empirical evidence."

"This research is critical to understanding the evolution of the skulls of modern and ancient snakes," added Caldwell.

The paper, "New Skulls and Skeletons of the Cretaceous Legged Snake Najash, and the Evolution of the Modern Snake Body Plan," was published in Science Advances in 2019.

Later studies added more twists to the snake origin story

Research published after the 2019 Najash paper has made the story even more interesting. In 2020, paleontologists described Boipeba tayasuensis, a Late Cretaceous blind snake from Brazil. That fossil pushed the record of blind snakes deeper into the age of dinosaurs and suggested that some early blind snakes were much larger than their living relatives, topping 1 meter in length. The finding supported the idea that parts of early snake evolution in Gondwana were more diverse, and often bigger-bodied, than once assumed.

Then, in 2023, another Science Advances study approached snake origins from an entirely different angle by reconstructing the brains of living squamates and fossil snakes. That work suggested the ancestor of crown snakes, meaning the group that gave rise to living snakes, may have been adapted for burrowing while also behaving opportunistically. Rather than neatly settling the debate, the result showed that snake origins were likely complex, with different branches of the snake family tree preserving different clues about how body shape, habitat, and feeding style evolved.

A 2025 Nature study added even more context by describing a Middle Jurassic squamate from Scotland with a striking mix of lizard-like and snake-like traits. The authors found that early squamate evolution involved a great deal of anatomical experimentation and convergent evolution, which helps explain why the earliest snake story has been so difficult to untangle from fossils alone.

Why Najash still matters

Even with those later discoveries, Najash remains one of the clearest windows into a crucial stage of snake evolution. It captures a moment when snakes still had hindlimbs, still retained a more lizard-like skull in some respects, and had not yet fully acquired the body plan seen in their modern descendants. That combination is exactly what makes the fossil so valuable. It does not just show an ancient snake. It shows an ancient snake in transition.

The University of Alberta described the work as part of the Faculty of Science's broader mission as a major center for research and teaching, with a focus on advancing knowledge through classroom, laboratory, and field research.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about snake evolution based on a remarkably preserved 100 million-year-old fossil. This finding reshapes understanding of snake origins, correcting long-standing anatomical misconceptions and providing new insights into evolutionary biology. The impact is broad for the scientific community and the general public interested in natural history and evolution, with detailed context and follow-up studies enhancing the story's substance.
articles.personality_title: 100-million-year-old snake fossil changes how we understand snake evolution
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long studied how snakes evolved from lizard-like ancestors, but many details about their early history remained unclear due to limited fossils.

**What happened** – Researchers studied a very well-preserved fossil of Najash rionegrina from Argentina, a snake that lived nearly 100 million years ago. This ancient snake still had small hind legs and a cheekbone, called the jugal bone, which modern snakes mostly lack. Using special scanning technology, scientists examined the skull in great detail, revealing important features that challenge older ideas about how snakes began.

**Impact** – The fossil shows that early snakes were likely large, wide-mouthed predators rather than tiny burrowers as previously thought. It also clarifies how snake skull bones changed over time, fixing a 160-year-old mistake about the jugal bone. This discovery helps explain the transition from lizard-like ancestors to the limbless snakes we see today.

**What’s next step** – Later studies continue to explore snake evolution with new fossils and brain studies, showing that snake origins were complex with many different adaptations. Scientists will keep studying fossils like Najash to better understand how snakes developed their unique body shapes and behaviors.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The Najash fossil provides a rare glimpse into a key stage of snake evolution, revealing that early snakes had legs and different skull bones, changing how we understand their origins.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un fossile de serpent vieux de 100 millions d’années change notre compréhension de l’évolution des serpents
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient depuis longtemps l’évolution des serpents à partir d’ancêtres ressemblant à des lézards, mais de nombreux détails restaient inconnus à cause du manque de fossiles.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont étudié un fossile très bien conservé de Najash rionegrina, un serpent qui vivait il y a près de 100 millions d’années en Argentine. Ce serpent ancien avait encore de petites pattes arrière et un os de la joue appelé os jugal, que les serpents modernes ont presque perdu. Grâce à un scanner spécial, les scientifiques ont examiné le crâne avec beaucoup de détails, révélant des caractéristiques importantes qui remettent en question les anciennes idées sur l’origine des serpents.

**Impact** – Le fossile montre que les premiers serpents étaient probablement de grands prédateurs à large bouche, et non de petits animaux fouisseurs comme on le pensait. Il corrige aussi une erreur vieille de 160 ans sur l’os jugal. Cette découverte aide à comprendre la transition des ancêtres ressemblant à des lézards vers les serpents sans membres d’aujourd’hui.

**Prochaines étapes** – Des études récentes poursuivent l’exploration de l’évolution des serpents avec de nouveaux fossiles et des analyses cérébrales, montrant que leurs origines sont complexes et variées. Les scientifiques continueront à étudier des fossiles comme Najash pour mieux comprendre comment les serpents ont développé leur forme et leurs comportements uniques.

**Une phrase clé** – Le fossile de Najash offre un aperçu rare d’une étape clé de l’évolution des serpents, montrant que les premiers serpents avaient des pattes et des os du crâne différents, ce qui change notre vision de leurs origines.
articles.personality_title_es: Un fósil de serpiente de 100 millones de años cambia cómo entendemos la evolución de las serpientes
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han estudiado durante mucho tiempo cómo evolucionaron las serpientes a partir de ancestros parecidos a lagartos, pero muchos detalles seguían siendo desconocidos por la falta de fósiles.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores estudiaron un fósil muy bien conservado de Najash rionegrina de Argentina, una serpiente que vivió hace casi 100 millones de años. Esta serpiente antigua todavía tenía pequeñas patas traseras y un hueso de la mejilla llamado hueso yugular, que las serpientes modernas casi han perdido. Usando tecnología especial de escaneo, examinaron el cráneo con gran detalle, revelando características importantes que desafían ideas antiguas sobre el origen de las serpientes.

**Impacto** – El fósil muestra que las primeras serpientes probablemente eran grandes depredadores de boca ancha, no pequeños animales que cavaban como se pensaba antes. También corrige un error de 160 años sobre el hueso yugular. Este descubrimiento ayuda a entender la transición de ancestros parecidos a lagartos a las serpientes sin patas que conocemos hoy.

**Próximo paso** – Estudios posteriores continúan explorando la evolución de las serpientes con nuevos fósiles y análisis cerebrales, mostrando que el origen de las serpientes fue complejo y con muchas adaptaciones diferentes. Los científicos seguirán estudiando fósiles como Najash para comprender mejor cómo las serpientes desarrollaron sus formas y comportamientos únicos.

**Una frase clave** – El fósil de Najash ofrece una rara mirada a una etapa clave de la evolución de las serpientes, mostrando que las primeras serpientes tenían patas y huesos del cráneo diferentes, cambiando cómo entendemos su origen.
articles.image_prompt: An intricately detailed painting of an ancient snake with visible small hind legs and a semi-transparent skull revealing a distinctive cheekbone (jugal bone), resting on a rocky outcrop in a softly lit Patagonian landscape, surrounded by subtle hints of ancient fossils embedded in the earth.

articles.title: Field re-entrant superconductivity in Eu-doped infinite-layer nickelates
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 23 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10547-yField re-entrant superconductivity in Eu-doped infinite-layer nickelates
articles.content: Intertwined superconducting and magnetic orders may give rise to exotic quantum phases1–7, including field-induced and re-entrant superconductivity8–10. However, such magnetism-enhanced superconductivity has remained elusive in superconductors with higher transition temperatures1–3. While infinite-layer nickelates represent a new class of unconventional superconductors11–20, the impact of rare-earth magnetism on superconducting properties remains largely unexplored. Here, we show that Eu-doped infinite-layer nickelate Sm 0.95−x Ca 0.05 Eu x NiO 2 exhibits a magnetic-field-induced re-entrant superconducting phase in the Eu-rich over-doped regime. Zero-resistance transport and high-field diamagnetic screening confirm the superconducting nature of this phase, which emerges after the initial suppression of low-field superconductivity and remains robust across a broad range of temperatures, fields and field orientations. In the same doping range, we observe nonlinear Hall transport and hysteretic magnetoresistance, indicating the unconventional nature of the re-entrant behaviour. While partially consistent with a compensation mechanism between the Eu-derived exchange field and the applied field, our data reveal pronounced deviations from this model at the highest-doping levels. Our findings establish infinite-layer nickelates as a fertile platform for exploring magnetically driven high-field superconductivity in strongly correlated oxides.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in superconductivity involving Eu-doped infinite-layer nickelates, demonstrating a novel magnetic-field-induced re-entrant superconducting phase. This discovery advances understanding of unconventional superconductors and could have broad implications for future quantum technologies and materials science, benefiting society at large.
articles.personality_title: Scientists discover new magnetic-field-triggered superconductivity in nickelates
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Superconductors are materials that can carry electricity without any resistance, usually at very low temperatures. Some special superconductors also show magnetic properties, but how these two features work together is still not fully understood. Infinite-layer nickelates are a new type of superconductor that scientists are studying to learn more about these effects.

**What happened** – Researchers found that when they added europium (Eu) to a nickelate material called SmNiO2, the material showed a surprising behavior. Normally, superconductivity weakens when a magnetic field is applied. But in this Eu-doped nickelate, the superconductivity disappeared at first and then came back again at higher magnetic fields. This “re-entrant” superconductivity was confirmed by measuring zero electrical resistance and strong magnetic responses.

**Impact** – This is the first time such a magnetic-field-driven return of superconductivity has been seen in nickelates with relatively high transition temperatures. It shows that the magnetic atoms from europium can interact with the material’s superconducting state in a unique way. This discovery helps scientists understand how magnetism and superconductivity can work together, which could lead to new materials with special properties for future technology.

**What's next step** – Scientists will continue to study these Eu-doped nickelates to understand exactly how the magnetic fields and europium atoms cause this re-entrant superconductivity. They will also explore other similar materials to see if this effect can be found elsewhere and how it might be used in practical devices like quantum computers or advanced electronics.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Adding europium to nickelate superconductors creates a surprising magnetic-field-driven superconducting state that opens new paths for material science research.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent une nouvelle supraconductivité déclenchée par champ magnétique dans les nickelates
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les supraconducteurs sont des matériaux capables de conduire l’électricité sans résistance, généralement à très basse température. Certains supraconducteurs spéciaux présentent aussi des propriétés magnétiques, mais la manière dont ces deux caractéristiques interagissent reste peu comprise. Les nickelates à couche infinie sont un nouveau type de supraconducteur étudié pour mieux comprendre ces effets.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont découvert qu’en ajoutant de l’europium (Eu) à un matériau nickelate appelé SmNiO2, le matériau montrait un comportement surprenant. Normalement, la supraconductivité faiblit lorsqu’un champ magnétique est appliqué. Mais dans ce nickelate dopé à l’europium, la supraconductivité disparaissait d’abord puis revenait à des champs magnétiques plus élevés. Cette supraconductivité « ré-entrante » a été confirmée par des mesures de résistance électrique nulle et de fortes réponses magnétiques.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois qu’un tel retour de la supraconductivité induit par un champ magnétique est observé dans des nickelates avec des températures de transition relativement élevées. Cela montre que les atomes magnétiques d’europium peuvent interagir de façon unique avec l’état supraconducteur du matériau. Cette découverte aide les scientifiques à mieux comprendre comment le magnétisme et la supraconductivité peuvent coexister, ce qui pourrait conduire à de nouveaux matériaux aux propriétés spéciales pour les technologies futures.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont continuer à étudier ces nickelates dopés à l’europium pour comprendre précisément comment les champs magnétiques et les atomes d’europium provoquent cette supraconductivité ré-entrante. Ils exploreront aussi d’autres matériaux similaires pour voir si cet effet peut être retrouvé ailleurs et comment il pourrait être utilisé dans des dispositifs pratiques comme les ordinateurs quantiques ou l’électronique avancée.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L’ajout d’europium aux supraconducteurs nickelates crée un état supraconducteur surprenant induit par champ magnétique, ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour la recherche en science des matériaux.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren nueva superconductividad activada por campo magnético en niquelatos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los superconductores son materiales que pueden conducir electricidad sin resistencia, generalmente a temperaturas muy bajas. Algunos superconductores especiales también muestran propiedades magnéticas, pero no se entiende completamente cómo funcionan juntas estas dos características. Los niquelatos de capa infinita son un nuevo tipo de superconductor que los científicos estudian para aprender más sobre estos efectos.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores encontraron que al añadir europio (Eu) a un material llamado SmNiO2, el material mostró un comportamiento sorprendente. Normalmente, la superconductividad se debilita cuando se aplica un campo magnético. Pero en este niquelato dopado con europio, la superconductividad desapareció primero y luego volvió a aparecer a campos magnéticos más altos. Esta superconductividad “reentrante” fue confirmada midiendo resistencia eléctrica cero y fuertes respuestas magnéticas.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que se observa este tipo de regreso de la superconductividad inducido por campo magnético en niquelatos con temperaturas de transición relativamente altas. Esto muestra que los átomos magnéticos del europio pueden interactuar de manera única con el estado superconductor del material. Este descubrimiento ayuda a los científicos a entender cómo el magnetismo y la superconductividad pueden trabajar juntos, lo que podría llevar a nuevos materiales con propiedades especiales para futuras tecnologías.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos seguirán estudiando estos niquelatos dopados con europio para entender exactamente cómo los campos magnéticos y los átomos de europio causan esta superconductividad reentrante. También explorarán otros materiales similares para ver si este efecto se encuentra en otros lugares y cómo podría usarse en dispositivos prácticos como computadoras cuánticas o electrónica avanzada.

**Resumen en una frase** – Añadir europio a superconductores de niquelatos crea un estado superconductor sorprendente activado por campos magnéticos, abriendo nuevos caminos para la investigación en ciencia de materiales.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of an abstract crystalline lattice glowing softly with intertwined threads of electric blue and deep red light weaving through it, symbolizing the coexistence of superconducting currents and magnetic fields within a layered oxide structure, set against a simple, muted background that highlights the delicate balance and re-entrant flow of energy through the Eu-doped infinite-layer nickelate.

articles.title: Gravity's strength measured more reliably than ever before
articles.summary: Measuring the strength of gravity is extraordinarily difficult, and different experiments have always disagreed – but a new test is paving the way to finally understanding nature’s most enigmatic force
articles.content:
Measuring the strength of gravity is extraordinarily difficult, and different experiments have always disagreed – but a new test is paving the way to finally understanding nature’s most enigmatic force

Stephan Schlamminger and his colleague, Vincent Lee, examine the torsion balance they used to measure the gravitational constant R. Eskalis/NIST

For centuries, physicists have been trying to measure the strength of gravity, a number called “big G”. The measurements have never lined up with one another, hinting that either we don’t fully understand our experiments or perhaps we don’t fully understand gravity. The latest test doesn’t confirm either of these scenarios – but the extraordinary precision and care taken in the newest big G experiment may finally bring researchers closer to a consensus.

Gravity is much weaker than the other fundamental forces, which makes it extraordinarily hard to measure it precisely. “As kids, we were all mesmerised when we played with magnets by the way they attract each other. The same is true of gravity – if you have two coffee cups and you put them in each hand, there is still a force between them, but it’s so small you can’t feel it, so you’re not as mesmerised,” says Stephan Schlamminger at the US National Institute of Standards and Technology in Maryland. That weakness is also part of what makes it so difficult to measure the true strength of gravity.

The other part is that, unlike the other forces, it is impossible to shield an experiment from gravity. In 1798, physicist Henry Cavendish got around this by using a device called a torsion balance, which enabled him to measure gravity for the first time, albeit with low precision.

To imagine a torsion balance, picture a horizontal toothpick hanging from a thread at its centre. At each end of the toothpick is a small marble. If you move another object near one of the marbles, that object’s gravity will attract the marble, causing the toothpick to turn slightly. By measuring the amount that the toothpick turns, you can calculate the strength of gravity between the marble and the outside object without worrying about Earth’s gravity, which is counteracted by the thread.

The experiment that Schlamminger and his colleagues performed was a much more sophisticated version of this, with eight weights set on two precisely calibrated turntables, all suspended by ribbons about as thick as a human hair. This was a painstaking reproduction of an experiment first performed in France in 2007. The researchers took a decade to measure and reduce every possible source of uncertainty. “This is experimental physics at its best,” says Jens Gundlach at the University of Washington, who wasn’t involved with this work.

Free newsletter Sign up to Maths Want to get a newsletter on everything mathematical? Register your interest and you’ll be one of the first to receive our maths newsletter when it launches. Sign up to newsletter

“The level of care that they have taken and all of the different effects that they have explored, this is a game-changer kind of experiment,” says Kasey Wagoner at North Carolina State University, who was also not involved with this work. The final value of big G was 6.67387×10-11 metres3 per kilogram per second2. That’s a fraction of a per cent lower than the 2007 measurement, but it is enough to bring the measurement more in line with other tests that have been performed over the years.

“Big G is not just a measurement of gravity – it’s a measurement of how well you can measure gravity, and it transcends epochs of physics. We can compare our experiment to Cavendish’s experiment 230 years ago, and in 230 years they’ll be able to compare theirs to ours,” says Schlamminger. “In the end, I think it will be about which era of humanity can measure this best, with the most agreement between the measurements.”

By pinning down several sources of uncertainty that weren’t previously known, Schlamminger and his team have increased that agreement, says Gundlach. “The landscape looks better now, more trustworthy, more reliable,” he says.

They have also paved the way for future experiments to measure big G even more precisely, which will become increasingly important as cosmological measurements – many of which rely on knowledge of gravity’s strength – also grow in precision. “If there’s something funny going on here, it’ll have effects all the way from the scale of the lab to the scale of the universe,” says Wagoner. “What is a very small, minute difference in the lab, when you put that on cosmic scales, that difference gets blown up, and it could have really big implications.”

While most researchers agree that the more likely explanation for the remaining discrepancy is that we do not fully understand the sources of bias and uncertainty in all of the experiments, there is a chance that it is actually due to gravity behaving differently from how we thought. If that is the case, it would hint at potential exotic new physics. “There is a crack in our understanding of science, and we have to go into these cracks – there may be nothing there, but it would be foolish not to go,” says Schlamminger.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific achievement: a highly precise and reliable measurement of the gravitational constant 'big G', resolving longstanding experimental discrepancies. This breakthrough has broad implications for physics and cosmology, potentially advancing our fundamental understanding of nature and enabling more accurate cosmological measurements. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a meaningful real-world impact in scientific knowledge.
articles.personality_title: Scientists achieve most reliable measurement of gravity’s strength
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For hundreds of years, scientists have tried to measure the strength of gravity, called the gravitational constant or “big G.” This number tells us how strong gravity is, but different experiments have given different answers. Gravity is very weak and hard to measure, making this a big challenge.

**What happened** – A team led by Stephan Schlamminger at the US National Institute of Standards and Technology used a highly sensitive device called a torsion balance. This tool measures tiny forces by seeing how much a suspended bar twists when objects attract each other. Their experiment took ten years to carefully reduce errors and was a detailed version of a 2007 test. They found a new value for big G that is more precise and closer to other recent experiments.

**Impact** – This new measurement is important because it helps scientists agree on the true strength of gravity, something that has been unclear for a long time. Better understanding big G is not just about one number—it also improves how we test physics over centuries. It will help scientists make more accurate measurements in space and better understand the universe, since gravity affects everything from small objects to entire galaxies.

**What’s next step** – Researchers will use this improved method to measure big G even more precisely. They will also explore whether small differences in measurements mean gravity behaves differently than we think, which could lead to new discoveries in physics. Future experiments will build on this work to better understand gravity and its role in the cosmos.

**One-sentence takeaway** – After years of effort, scientists have made the most reliable measurement yet of gravity’s strength, helping to settle a long-standing mystery and improve our understanding of the universe.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques réalisent la mesure la plus fiable de la force de gravité
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis des centaines d’années, les scientifiques tentent de mesurer la force de la gravité, appelée constante gravitationnelle ou « grand G ». Ce nombre indique la force de la gravité, mais différentes expériences ont donné des résultats différents. La gravité est très faible et difficile à mesurer, ce qui rend cette tâche compliquée.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe dirigée par Stephan Schlamminger au National Institute of Standards and Technology aux États-Unis a utilisé un appareil très sensible appelé balance de torsion. Cet outil mesure de très petites forces en observant la torsion d’une barre suspendue lorsque des objets s’attirent. Leur expérience, qui a duré dix ans pour réduire les erreurs, est une version détaillée d’un test réalisé en 2007. Ils ont trouvé une nouvelle valeur pour le grand G, plus précise et plus proche des autres mesures récentes.

**Impact** – Cette nouvelle mesure est importante car elle aide les scientifiques à s’accorder sur la véritable force de la gravité, ce qui restait incertain depuis longtemps. Mieux comprendre le grand G ne concerne pas qu’un seul chiffre : cela améliore aussi la façon dont nous testons la physique à travers les siècles. Cela aidera les scientifiques à faire des mesures plus précises dans l’espace et à mieux comprendre l’univers, car la gravité influence tout, des petits objets aux galaxies entières.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs utiliseront cette méthode améliorée pour mesurer le grand G avec encore plus de précision. Ils étudieront aussi si de petites différences dans les mesures signifient que la gravité se comporte différemment de ce que l’on pense, ce qui pourrait conduire à de nouvelles découvertes en physique. Les futures expériences s’appuieront sur ce travail pour mieux comprendre la gravité et son rôle dans le cosmos.

**En une phrase** – Après des années d’efforts, les scientifiques ont réalisé la mesure la plus fiable à ce jour de la force de gravité, aidant à résoudre un mystère de longue date et à mieux comprendre l’univers.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos logran la medición más fiable de la fuerza de la gravedad
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante cientos de años, los científicos han intentado medir la fuerza de la gravedad, llamada constante gravitacional o “gran G”. Este número nos dice qué tan fuerte es la gravedad, pero diferentes experimentos han dado respuestas distintas. La gravedad es muy débil y difícil de medir, lo que hace que esta tarea sea complicada.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo dirigido por Stephan Schlamminger en el Instituto Nacional de Estándares y Tecnología de Estados Unidos usó un dispositivo muy sensible llamado balanza de torsión. Esta herramienta mide fuerzas muy pequeñas al observar cuánto gira una barra suspendida cuando los objetos se atraen. Su experimento duró diez años para reducir errores y fue una versión detallada de una prueba realizada en 2007. Encontraron un nuevo valor para gran G, más preciso y más cercano a otras mediciones recientes.

**Impacto** – Esta nueva medición es importante porque ayuda a los científicos a ponerse de acuerdo sobre la verdadera fuerza de la gravedad, algo que no estaba claro desde hace mucho tiempo. Entender mejor el gran G no es solo un número: también mejora cómo probamos la física a lo largo del tiempo. Esto ayudará a los científicos a hacer mediciones más precisas en el espacio y a comprender mejor el universo, ya que la gravedad afecta todo, desde objetos pequeños hasta galaxias enteras.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores usarán este método mejorado para medir gran G con aún más precisión. También explorarán si pequeñas diferencias en las mediciones significan que la gravedad se comporta diferente de lo que pensamos, lo que podría llevar a nuevos descubrimientos en física. Los futuros experimentos se basarán en este trabajo para entender mejor la gravedad y su papel en el cosmos.

**Frase clave** – Tras años de esfuerzo, los científicos han logrado la medición más fiable hasta ahora de la fuerza de la gravedad, ayudando a resolver un misterio antiguo y a mejorar nuestra comprensión del universo.
articles.image_prompt: A warm-toned detailed painting of a delicate torsion balance with thin ribbons suspending precisely arranged weights on calibrated turntables, gently turning against a softly glowing abstract cosmic background symbolizing the universe, capturing the meticulous measurement of gravity’s subtle force.

articles.title: QBox theory may offer glimpse of reality deeper than quantum realm
articles.summary: Physicists have long suspected that there is a layer of physical reality beneath quantum theory and a new mathematical model unveils just how strange it might be
articles.content:
Physicists have long suspected that there is a layer of physical reality beneath quantum theory and a new mathematical model unveils just how strange it might be

There may be a layer of reality even deeper than the quantum realm Cappan/iStockphoto/Getty Images

Physicists are making new inroads into the world of post-quantum theories, uncovering what reality may look like on a level deeper and stranger than the already infamously odd quantum theory.

In the 1920s, physicists had several extremely useful theories for how the world works, yet they kept uncovering situations where those theories didn’t work. Through these holes in so-called classical physics, they glimpsed a deeper layer of the world that underlies everything – the quantum realm. Now, physicists are having déjà vu. Quantum theory works incredibly well, but it also has gaping holes when confronted with cosmically large objects controlled by gravity. What sort of post-quantum world could reveal itself through that hole?

James Hefford at the National Institute for Research in Digital Science and Technology and Matt Wilson at Paris-Saclay University, both in France, have now developed a mathematical sketch of one plausible post-quantum world, possibly the deepest layer of reality yet.

“Quantum theory doesn’t describe the entire universe,” says Hefford. “One of the greatest problems in physics is to come up with a theory of quantum gravity, a theory that describes both quantum theory and gravity. That thing ought to somehow go beyond just quantum theory.”

There are many proposals for how to develop a theory of quantum gravity, but Wilson and Hefford took inspiration from the relationship between quantum and classical physics. Specifically, we do not encounter strange quantum effects in everyday life because of a process called decoherence, which destroys most objects’ quantumness. It is thanks to decoherence that our very reasonable and tangible world emerges from the quantum world in which cats can seemingly be simultaneously dead and alive, and particles can tunnel through walls like ghosts. The researchers posited that an analogous process of “hyperdecoherence” ought to make quantum theory emerge from a post-quantum one.

Free newsletter Sign up to The Weekly The best of New Scientist, including long-reads, culture, podcasts and news, each week. Sign up to newsletter

The idea had been studied before, but a specific theorem from 2018 had proved it to be mathematically impossible to come up with a sensible and internally consistent process of hyperdecoherence that would correctly reproduce quantum theory. Hefford and Wilson carefully studied the assumptions that underlie this theorem and devised a workaround. The price they paid was that they ended up in a very strange post-quantum realm: a theory called QBox.

One of its key features is that it bends the conventional idea of causality. Conventionally, either event A causes event B or vice versa, but in QBox, it is allowable to have mixtures of “A causes B” and “B causes A” where it is impossible to tell which one is unambiguously correct.

“This is causal indefiniteness. We should care about it if we want to pursue a theory of quantum gravity,” says Carlo Maria Scandolo at the University of Calgary in Canada, who did not work on the project. This is because our best theory of gravity – Albert Einstein’s general relativity – imposes different orders of cause-and-effect at different points of space-time, he says.

This manifests itself, for instance, in thought experiments where people travelling in different spaceships observing the same set of events can’t agree on the order in which they occurred.

The two physicists also had to make sure that hyperdecoherence reduces QBox to quantum theory correctly. For example, they had to make sure that a given object that we can only know approximately in QBox does not become more clearly known after hyperdecohering. Wilson says that the hyperdecoherence process is akin to there being dimensions that an agent operating in the QBox realm – someone who can interact with objects within it – can access, but that become hidden to those of us who can only access the quantum or classical realms.

The two researchers are still working out the details of how to think of these dimensions, and what exactly the agent would experience, but the dimensions that become inaccessible seem to be temporal rather than spatial, with hyperdecoherence cutting off access to processes that happen backwards, going into the past rather than the future.

“There have [previously] been a few toy theories that would support notions of indefinite causal order and so forth, but getting them to then reproduce all of quantum mechanics was a challenging thing that no one seemed to have really kind of gotten right,” says Ciarán Gilligan-Lee at the Causal Inference Research Lab at Spotify who had co-authored the 2018 theorem arguing against hyperdecoherence. He says that it is a real strength of the new work that it is a concrete theory and also fairly mathematically minimal. Despite some of its oddities, QBox does not require making up a whole new world of objects, such as cosmic strings, to get to a theory of quantum gravity, he says.

An important next step is to flesh out the physical details of hyperdecoherence, in addition to proving that it can exist as a mathematical function, says John Selby at the University of Gdańsk in Poland, who was the other co-author of the 2018 theorem. “There should be some narrative, some story that says why in our current experiments this is [what is] happening,” he says. In his view, even if QBox turns out to not be exactly what the post-quantum layer of reality is like, Hefford and Wilson’s mathematical work is a promising starting point.

Gilligan-Lee and Selby have also drafted a new and different theorem that has not yet been reviewed by other physicists but that may put more stringent constraints on what it would mean for a theory like QBox to meaningfully decohere to quantum theory.

Such challenges are welcome, even if they mean that QBox ends up being a stepping stone towards a better wish list for what a post-quantum theory ought to be, says Wilson. Remarkably, experimental tests of QBox may also eventually be possible because the theory could have concrete implications for certain experiments where quantum waves overlap.

And if QBox does make it through future mathematical and experimental gauntlets, an even more tantalising question will become relevant. “Can you have whole towers of theories decohering into each other by similar [decoherence] mechanisms?” says Hefford. The quest for the deepest layer of reality may involve some more mathematical digging after all.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in theoretical physics with the development of the QBox model, a plausible post-quantum theory that could deepen our understanding of reality and quantum gravity. This advancement has broad implications for fundamental physics and may eventually lead to experimental tests, representing meaningful progress in science and technology with potential large-scale impact.
articles.personality_title: New QBox theory reveals a stranger layer beneath quantum physics
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have known for nearly a century that quantum physics explains very small things like atoms and particles, but it doesn’t fully explain how gravity works on a huge scale, like with stars and galaxies. Physicists suspect there might be a deeper layer of reality beyond quantum physics that could connect these ideas.

**What happened** – Two physicists in France, James Hefford and Matt Wilson, created a new mathematical model called QBox. This model imagines a world even stranger than the quantum world. It allows events to happen without a clear cause-and-effect order, meaning sometimes it’s unclear which event caused the other. They designed QBox so that quantum physics would appear naturally from it through a process they call "hyperdecoherence."

**Impact** – QBox is important because it offers a fresh way to think about how the universe might work at its deepest level, including how gravity and quantum physics might fit together. Unlike earlier ideas, QBox does not need new kinds of objects or forces, making it simpler and more realistic. It also matches some strange ideas from Einstein’s theory of gravity, where cause and effect can change depending on where you are in space and time.

**What's next step** – The researchers need to better understand how hyperdecoherence works in the real world and what it would feel like to exist in the QBox world. Other scientists are checking the math and exploring if experiments could one day test QBox. This could help prove if QBox or a similar theory is true and guide the search for an even deeper understanding of reality.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The QBox model offers a new, mathematically sound way to explore a deeper level of reality beyond quantum physics, bringing scientists closer to uniting quantum theory and gravity.
articles.personality_title_fr: La théorie QBox dévoile un niveau plus étrange que la physique quantique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis près d’un siècle, les scientifiques savent que la physique quantique explique très bien les petites choses comme les atomes, mais elle n’explique pas complètement la gravité à grande échelle, comme pour les étoiles et les galaxies. Les physiciens pensent qu’il pourrait exister un niveau plus profond de réalité au-delà de la physique quantique qui relierait ces idées.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Deux physiciens en France, James Hefford et Matt Wilson, ont créé un nouveau modèle mathématique appelé QBox. Ce modèle imagine un monde encore plus étrange que le monde quantique. Il permet que des événements se produisent sans un ordre clair de cause à effet, ce qui signifie qu’il est parfois impossible de dire quel événement a causé l’autre. Ils ont conçu QBox pour que la physique quantique apparaisse naturellement grâce à un processus qu’ils appellent « hyperdécohérence ».

**Impact** – QBox est important car il offre une nouvelle façon de penser le fonctionnement de l’univers à son niveau le plus profond, y compris comment la gravité et la physique quantique peuvent s’accorder. Contrairement aux idées précédentes, QBox n’a pas besoin de nouveaux types d’objets ou de forces, ce qui le rend plus simple et plus réaliste. Il correspond aussi à certaines idées étranges de la théorie de la gravité d’Einstein, où cause et effet peuvent changer selon l’endroit dans l’espace et le temps.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs doivent mieux comprendre comment fonctionne l’hyperdécohérence dans le monde réel et ce que ressentirait un être vivant dans le monde QBox. D’autres scientifiques vérifient les mathématiques et explorent si des expériences pourraient un jour tester QBox. Cela pourrait aider à prouver si QBox ou une théorie similaire est vraie et guider la recherche d’une compréhension encore plus profonde de la réalité.

**En une phrase** – Le modèle QBox propose une nouvelle approche mathématique fiable pour explorer un niveau plus profond de la réalité au-delà de la physique quantique, rapprochant les scientifiques de l’unification de la théorie quantique et de la gravité.
articles.personality_title_es: La teoría QBox revela un nivel más extraño que la física cuántica
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Desde hace casi un siglo, los científicos saben que la física cuántica explica muy bien las cosas pequeñas como los átomos, pero no explica completamente la gravedad a gran escala, como en estrellas y galaxias. Los físicos sospechan que podría existir un nivel más profundo de la realidad más allá de la física cuántica que conecte estas ideas.

**Qué pasó** – Dos físicos en Francia, James Hefford y Matt Wilson, crearon un nuevo modelo matemático llamado QBox. Este modelo imagina un mundo aún más extraño que el mundo cuántico. Permite que los eventos ocurran sin un orden claro de causa y efecto, lo que significa que a veces no se puede decir cuál evento causó al otro. Diseñaron QBox para que la física cuántica aparezca naturalmente a través de un proceso llamado "hiperdecoherencia."

**Impacto** – QBox es importante porque ofrece una forma nueva de pensar cómo funciona el universo en su nivel más profundo, incluyendo cómo la gravedad y la física cuántica pueden unirse. A diferencia de ideas anteriores, QBox no necesita nuevos tipos de objetos o fuerzas, lo que lo hace más simple y realista. También coincide con algunas ideas extrañas de la teoría de la gravedad de Einstein, donde la causa y efecto pueden cambiar según dónde estés en el espacio y tiempo.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores deben entender mejor cómo funciona la hiperdecoherencia en el mundo real y qué sentiría alguien que existiera en el mundo QBox. Otros científicos están revisando las matemáticas y explorando si algún día podrían hacer experimentos para probar QBox. Esto podría ayudar a demostrar si QBox o una teoría similar es correcta y guiar la búsqueda de una comprensión aún más profunda de la realidad.

**Una frase clave** – El modelo QBox ofrece una nueva manera matemática y sólida para explorar un nivel más profundo de la realidad más allá de la física cuántica, acercando a los científicos a unir la teoría cuántica y la gravedad.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of an abstract cosmic landscape featuring intertwined translucent spheres representing quantum and post-quantum realms, connected by a swirling ribbon of light symbolizing hyperdecoherence, with overlapping glowing arrows bending and looping to illustrate indefinite causality, all rendered in soft natural hues of blues, purples, and warm golds to evoke a sense of profound mystery and hopeful discovery.
